refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable execution of the load→travel→dump cycle within the customer-defined cycle time target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting/enabling the cycle execution capability, and (2) achieving a measured KPI (p95 ≤ CT_target). The refined FR focuses on the functional requirement—enabling the cycle to be executed within the target duration—which is solution-neutral and measurable. The acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ CT_target under duty cycle and site conditions) is a verification/validation gate, not part of the FR itself. This separation clarifies that the FR is about functional capability, while the acceptance criterion is about performance validation under specified conditions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure the p95 cycle time across repeated load→travel→dump cycles under the agreed duty cycle and site conditions. Verify that p95 ≤ CT_target. This is a quantitative acceptance test, not a functional requirement.
sub_frs: The NGHE solution shall enable the load phase to be completed within the customer-defined load time target.
The NGHE solution shall enable the travel phase to be completed within the customer-defined travel time target.
The NGHE solution shall enable the dump phase to be completed within the customer-defined dump time target.

candidate_dps: DP1: Decompose cycle into discrete, sequenced phases (load, travel, dump) with phase-gating logic that enforces strict ordering and allows independent optimization of each phase duration.

DP2: Implement a centralized cycle-time budget allocation mechanism that distributes CT_target across phases, with real-time monitoring and adaptive phase-duration adjustment to maintain p95 compliance.

DP3: Design a predictive cycle-time model that accounts for site conditions (terrain, load mass, distance) and pre-computes optimal phase parameters before cycle execution, with fallback to nominal timing.

DP4: Establish a closed-loop feedback control system that measures actual phase durations against budgeted targets and applies corrective actions (speed modulation, path optimization) within each phase.

DP5: Decouple cycle-time performance from external variability (site conditions, load variance) by introducing a buffering or scheduling layer that absorbs variance and guarantees deterministic phase transitions.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP2 + DP4 (integrated): Implement a centralized cycle-time budget allocation mechanism that distributes CT_target across load, travel, and dump phases with explicit phase-duration targets. Pair this with closed-loop feedback control that monitors actual phase durations in real-time, detects deviations from budget, and applies corrective actions (e.g., speed adjustment, path re-optimization) to maintain p95 ≤ CT_target under site variability. This design separates budget planning (DP2) from execution control (DP4), enabling independent tuning of allocation strategy and feedback responsiveness.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between phase-duration targets and site-condition models: if terrain/load prediction is inaccurate, budget allocation fails. Mitigation: use conservative (pessimistic) initial budgets and adaptive re-allocation.

Feedback control loop responsiveness vs. phase duration: if corrective actions (e.g., speed change) have long latency relative to phase duration, control may be ineffective. Mitigation: ensure actuation bandwidth is ≥ 3× fastest phase duration.

Cross-phase dependencies: dump phase may be blocked if travel phase overruns; load phase may be blocked if dump phase is slow. Mitigation: design phases as independent state machines with explicit handoff protocols.
Measurement coupling: if cycle-time sensors are unreliable or have high latency, feedback control becomes unstable. Mitigation: specify sensor accuracy and latency requirements explicitly.
coupling_check.notes: DP2+DP4 avoids monolithic cycle design and supports independent optimization of allocation vs. control. However, success depends critically on accurate site-condition modeling and low-latency feedback. DP1 (phase decomposition) is a prerequisite; DP3 (predictive modeling) is a supporting enabler.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Site conditions (terrain, load mass, distance) are measurable or predictable with sufficient accuracy to inform initial budget allocation. RISK: If site variability is high and unpredictable, budget allocation will be conservative, reducing throughput. MITIGATION: Implement adaptive re-allocation based on first few cycles.

ASSUMPTION: Actuation system (drive motors, hydraulics, steering) can respond to corrective commands (speed, path changes) with latency ≤ 10% of phase duration. RISK: If latency is high, feedback control cannot correct deviations within a single phase. MITIGATION: Specify actuation bandwidth in DP realization; consider predictive control if latency is unavoidable.

ASSUMPTION: Cycle-time measurement (via sensors, GPS, or odometry) is accurate to ±5% of CT_target. RISK: If measurement noise is high, feedback control will oscillate or diverge. MITIGATION: Implement low-pass filtering and deadband logic in feedback loop.

ASSUMPTION: Load, travel, and dump phases can be executed sequentially without blocking or resource contention. RISK: If phases share actuators or hydraulic circuits, phase transitions may be delayed. MITIGATION: Validate phase independence in DP realization; design hydraulic/electrical architecture to support concurrent actuation if needed.

ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined CT_target is achievable under stated duty cycle and site conditions. RISK: If CT_target is unrealistic (e.g., requires speeds exceeding safety limits), no DP can satisfy FR. MITIGATION: Validate CT_target feasibility during requirements review; document assumptions about load mass, distance, and terrain.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan does not impose hard constraints that conflict with cycle-time optimization (e.g., mandatory speed limits that make CT_target unachievable). RISK: Safety constraints may force conservative phase durations. MITIGATION: Coordinate with safety team early; ensure DP respects all safety constraints.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) the acceptable range of site conditions (load mass, distance, terrain type) under which p95 ≤ CT_target must hold; (2) the measurement method and accuracy for cycle-time validation; (3) the priority order if CT_target conflicts with safety or operational constraints. Decompose DP2+DP4 into two separate FRs: FR2a (allocate cycle-time budget across phases) and FR2b (control phase durations in real-time to maintain budget compliance). This separation clarifies ownership and enables independent testing of budget allocation vs. feedback control. Add explicit IM layer: IM2a (cycle-time budget module, owned by planning/scheduling team) and IM2b (feedback control module, owned by vehicle control team), with a well-defined interface (phase-duration targets and actual measurements).

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized cycle-time budget allocation engine and closed-loop feedback control system that owns budget planning logic, phase-duration targets, real-time deviation detection, and corrective action orchestration across load, travel, and dump phases.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives real-time phase duration observations, actual speeds, path metrics, and site variability signals from Vehicle to enable closed-loop feedback control and deviation detection.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Sends corrective action directives (speed adjustments, path re-optimization commands, phase-duration target updates) to Vehicle and Teleoperation to maintain p95 ≤ CT_target.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints that bound corrective actions; sends alerts if budget allocation or feedback control decisions conflict with safety thresholds.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. NGHE Solution is the natural home for mission-level optimization logic, budget allocation strategy, and closed-loop control orchestration. Budget planning (DP2) and feedback control (DP4) are both algorithmic/decision-making functions that belong in the planning/optimization layer. Minimal diffusion: only Telemetry (input) and Tasking (output) cross boundary; SafetyAlert is a constraint interface. No duplication of budget or control logic needed elsewhere.

Poor fit. Vehicle is a physical/execution platform, not a planning or control orchestration layer. Budget allocation and closed-loop feedback control are mission-level concerns that should not reside in the vehicle module. Placing DP2+DP4 here would scatter control logic across vehicle firmware, creating tight coupling to hardware and making strategy tuning difficult. High diffusion: corrective actions would need to propagate back to planning, and budget logic would be embedded in vehicle code.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is a human-in-the-loop interface layer, not a planning or autonomous control engine. Budget allocation and closed-loop feedback control are autonomous decision-making functions that belong in NGHE Solution, not in the teleoperation module. Placing DP2+DP4 here would conflate human guidance with algorithmic optimization, reducing autonomy and creating confusion about control authority. High diffusion: budget and feedback logic would need to be replicated or coordinated across multiple modules.

Poor fit. Safety Services is a constraint/validation layer, not a planning or control orchestration layer. Budget allocation and closed-loop feedback control are mission optimization functions, not safety enforcement. Placing DP2+DP4 here would blur the boundary between safety constraints and mission optimization, making it difficult to tune control responsiveness independently of safety logic. High diffusion: budget and feedback logic would need to be coordinated with NGHE Solution, creating circular dependencies.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the optimal boundary for DP2+DP4 because it is the designated mission-level planning and optimization layer. Budget allocation (DP2) is a strategic planning function that determines phase-duration targets based on CT_target and site constraints. Closed-loop feedback control (DP4) is an autonomous execution-monitoring function that detects deviations and orchestrates corrective actions. Both are algorithmic/decision-making concerns that belong in the planning domain, not in physical execution (Vehicle), human guidance (Teleoperation), or constraint validation (Safety Services). Containment is strong because budget and control logic can be fully encapsulated within NGHE Solution's optimization engine, with clean interfaces to Telemetry (input) and Tasking (output). Separation of DP2 (budget planning) from DP4 (feedback control) within NGHE Solution enables independent tuning of allocation strategy and feedback responsiveness without architectural diffusion.
containment_risks: Risk: If Telemetry interface is delayed or lossy, closed-loop feedback control (DP4) may not detect deviations in time to apply corrective actions. Mitigation: Define Telemetry SLA (latency, completeness) as a constraint on DP4 responsiveness.

Risk: If Tasking interface has high latency or low reliability, corrective actions may not reach Vehicle in time to prevent CT_target violation. Mitigation: Define Tasking SLA and fallback mechanisms (e.g., Vehicle-local speed governors) to ensure safety.

Risk: If SafetyAlert constraints are overly restrictive, corrective actions may be blocked, preventing DP4 from maintaining p95 ≤ CT_target. Mitigation: Coordinate budget allocation (DP2) with Safety Services to ensure feasible phase-duration targets.

Risk: If site variability is not fully captured in Telemetry, budget allocation (DP2) may be based on incomplete information, leading to infeasible targets. Mitigation: Ensure Telemetry includes all relevant variability signals (soil conditions, traffic, weather, etc.).

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion 'p95 ≤ CT_target under agreed duty cycle and site conditions' is directly measurable via Telemetry interface (phase duration observations, actual speeds, path metrics). NGHE Solution's closed-loop feedback control (DP4) can be instrumented to log deviations, corrective actions applied, and resulting cycle times. Verification requires: (1) instrumentation of Telemetry to capture phase durations with sufficient granularity and latency (<100ms recommended for real-time feedback), (2) logging of budget allocation decisions (DP2) and corrective action directives (DP4) for post-hoc analysis, (3) statistical aggregation of cycle times across multiple runs to compute p95 under site variability. Feasibility is high because NGHE Solution owns both the budget logic and feedback control, enabling end-to-end traceability from FR to measured outcome.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. The FR→DP→IM chain is clear and traceable: FR (enable load→travel→dump cycle within CT_target) maps to DP (centralized budget allocation + closed-loop feedback control) maps to IM (NGHE Solution module with Telemetry input, Tasking output, SafetyAlert constraint). The separation of DP2 (budget planning) from DP4 (feedback control) within NGHE Solution is architecturally sound and enables independent tuning. Phase-duration targets are explicit outputs of DP2 that feed into DP4's deviation detection logic. Corrective actions (speed adjustment, path re-optimization) are explicit outputs of DP4 that flow through Tasking interface to Vehicle. No ambiguity about ownership or responsibility boundaries.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Budget allocation logic (DP2) could diffuse into Vehicle firmware if phase-duration targets are hard-coded or locally optimized at the vehicle level. Mitigation: Enforce that all phase-duration targets originate from NGHE Solution and are transmitted via Tasking interface; Vehicle firmware must not override or locally recompute targets.

Risk: Feedback control logic (DP4) could diffuse if Vehicle implements local speed governors or path-following corrections that duplicate NGHE Solution's deviation detection. Mitigation: Define clear separation—Vehicle executes directives from NGHE Solution; any local safety governors must be passive (e.g., hard speed limits) and not attempt to optimize cycle time.

Risk: Corrective action orchestration (DP4) could diffuse if Teleoperation module is allowed to issue ad-hoc speed or path adjustments that conflict with NGHE Solution's feedback control. Mitigation: Establish control authority hierarchy—NGHE Solution has primary authority for cycle-time optimization; Teleoperation can override only for safety or manual intervention, with explicit logging.

Risk: SafetyAlert constraint interface could become a diffusion point if Safety Services imposes phase-duration constraints that are not coordinated with DP2's budget allocation. Mitigation: Require Safety Services to provide constraint bounds (e.g., max speed, min stopping distance) early in design; DP2 must validate that allocated phase-duration targets are feasible within these bounds.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Containment score of 0.92 for NGHE Solution is well-justified. Budget allocation (DP2) and closed-loop feedback control (DP4) are both algorithmic/decision-making functions that naturally belong in the mission-level planning and optimization layer. NGHE Solution is the designated owner of cycle-time optimization logic, and the module boundary is clean: Telemetry (input) provides observability, Tasking (output) provides actuation, SafetyAlert (constraint) provides safety bounds. No duplication of budget or control logic is needed elsewhere. The low containment scores for Vehicle (0.35), Teleoperation (0.28), and Safety Services (0.22) correctly reflect that these modules are execution, human-guidance, and constraint-validation layers respectively, not planning layers. Minimal diffusion risk if interfaces are strictly enforced.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP (centralized budget allocation + closed-loop feedback control) directly addresses FR (enable load→travel→dump cycle within CT_target). DP2 (budget allocation) ensures that phase-duration targets are set strategically based on CT_target and site constraints, decomposing the mission-level requirement into executable phase targets. DP4 (closed-loop feedback control) ensures that actual execution is monitored in real-time, deviations are detected, and corrective actions are applied to maintain p95 ≤ CT_target under site variability. The separation of planning (DP2) from execution monitoring (DP4) is sound and enables independent tuning of allocation strategy and feedback responsiveness. The DP is sufficiently detailed to guide implementation: it specifies the mechanism (centralized budget allocation engine), the feedback loop (real-time phase duration monitoring, deviation detection, corrective action orchestration), and the corrective actions (speed adjustment, path re-optimization). Acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ CT_target) is directly measurable as an outcome of DP execution.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Explicitly define Telemetry SLA (latency, completeness, accuracy) as a constraint on DP4 responsiveness. Specify maximum acceptable latency for phase-duration observations (e.g., <100ms) to ensure closed-loop feedback control can detect and correct deviations before they accumulate into CT_target violations.

Revision 2: Explicitly define Tasking SLA (latency, reliability) and fallback mechanisms. Specify maximum acceptable latency for corrective action directives (e.g., <200ms) and define fallback behavior if Tasking interface fails (e.g., Vehicle-local speed governors activate to prevent safety violations).

Revision 3: Establish a formal coordination protocol between DP2 (budget allocation) and Safety Services. Require that phase-duration targets computed by DP2 are validated against safety constraint bounds (max speed, min stopping distance, etc.) before being transmitted to Vehicle. Document the validation logic and any infeasibility resolution strategy.

Revision 4: Define the control authority hierarchy explicitly. Specify that NGHE Solution has primary authority for cycle-time optimization via DP2+DP4; Teleoperation can override only for safety or manual intervention, with explicit logging and audit trail. Vehicle firmware must not attempt to locally optimize cycle time or override NGHE Solution directives.

Revision 5: Specify the site variability signals that must be captured in Telemetry to enable accurate budget allocation (DP2). Examples: soil conditions (firmness, slope), traffic patterns, weather (rain, wind), load characteristics (weight, center of gravity). Ensure Telemetry interface includes all relevant variability signals; if any are missing, document the impact on DP2 accuracy.

Revision 6: Add explicit instrumentation and logging requirements for DP2 and DP4. Specify that NGHE Solution must log: (1) budget allocation decisions (phase-duration targets, allocation rationale), (2) real-time phase duration observations, (3) detected deviations and their magnitude, (4) corrective actions applied and their timing, (5) resulting cycle times. Enable post-hoc analysis and continuous improvement of allocation strategy and feedback control tuning.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, DP-IM, FR-DP) pass with high confidence, and containment analysis shows strong architectural coherence. However, the recommended_revisions identify six critical gaps that must be resolved before proceeding to sub-FR decomposition. These are not minor clarifications—they address: (1) interface SLAs that directly impact DP4 responsiveness and safety, (2) formal coordination with Safety Services that affects feasibility of DP2 budget allocation, (3) control authority hierarchy that prevents diffusion risks, (4) site variability instrumentation that enables DP2 accuracy, and (5) logging/instrumentation requirements that enable verification. Given high safety criticality, these revisions must be incorporated into the current-level FR-DP-IM specification before decomposing into sub-FRs. Proceeding without these revisions risks creating sub-FRs that inherit ambiguity about interface contracts, safety coordination, and verification strategy.

revise.why: DP2 (centralized budget allocation) and DP4 (closed-loop feedback control) are sound in principle but lack critical operational constraints and coordination mechanisms. Revisions 1-6 must be incorporated into the DP specification: (1) Telemetry SLA (latency <100ms, completeness, accuracy) as a hard constraint on DP4 design; (2) Tasking SLA (latency <200ms, reliability, fallback) as a hard constraint on DP4 responsiveness; (3) Formal validation protocol between DP2 and Safety Services to ensure phase-duration targets are feasible within safety bounds; (4) Explicit control authority hierarchy to prevent Vehicle or Teleoperation from overriding DP2+DP4 decisions; (5) Specification of site variability signals required by DP2 (soil, traffic, weather, load characteristics) to ensure budget allocation accuracy; (6) Instrumentation and logging requirements for DP2 and DP4 to enable verification and continuous improvement. These revisions strengthen the DP specification without changing its fundamental logic; they make the DP executable and verifiable.

assumptions: Telemetry interface can be instrumented to provide phase-duration observations with latency <100ms and sufficient granularity to detect deviations before they accumulate into CT_target violations.
Tasking interface can deliver corrective action directives (speed adjustment, path re-optimization) with latency <200ms and high reliability.
Safety Services will provide constraint bounds (max speed, min stopping distance, etc.) early in design, enabling DP2 to validate phase-duration targets for feasibility.
Vehicle firmware will not attempt to locally optimize cycle time or override NGHE Solution directives; any local governors are passive (hard limits) and do not conflict with DP2+DP4 logic.
Teleoperation module will respect control authority hierarchy and override NGHE Solution only for safety or manual intervention, with explicit logging.
Site variability signals (soil conditions, traffic patterns, weather, load characteristics) are observable via Telemetry and can be incorporated into DP2's budget allocation logic.
NGHE Solution has sufficient computational capacity to execute DP2 (budget allocation) and DP4 (closed-loop feedback control) in real-time without introducing unacceptable latency.
open_risks: Risk: Telemetry latency exceeds 100ms, preventing DP4 from detecting and correcting deviations in time. Mitigation: Establish Telemetry SLA as a hard requirement; if latency cannot be met, DP4 must be redesigned with predictive models or Vehicle-local governors to compensate.

Risk: Tasking latency exceeds 200ms, delaying corrective action delivery and allowing deviations to accumulate. Mitigation: Establish Tasking SLA as a hard requirement; if latency cannot be met, define fallback mechanisms (e.g., Vehicle-local speed governors activate automatically).

Risk: Safety Services imposes phase-duration constraints that are incompatible with CT_target, making DP2 infeasible. Mitigation: Establish formal coordination protocol early; if infeasibility is discovered, escalate to stakeholders for CT_target or safety constraint negotiation.

Risk: Vehicle firmware or Teleoperation module implements local cycle-time optimization that conflicts with DP2+DP4, causing oscillation or unpredictable behavior. Mitigation: Enforce control authority hierarchy via interface contracts and code review; implement audit logging to detect unauthorized overrides.

Risk: Site variability signals are not observable via Telemetry (e.g., soil firmness, load center of gravity), reducing DP2 accuracy and increasing p95 variance. Mitigation: Identify missing signals early; either add instrumentation or adjust DP2 to use proxy signals or conservative assumptions.

Risk: Instrumentation and logging overhead reduces NGHE Solution performance or introduces unacceptable latency. Mitigation: Design logging as asynchronous, non-blocking; use sampling or aggregation to reduce data volume; validate performance impact before deployment.
evidence_needed: Telemetry interface specification: latency, completeness, accuracy, sampling rate, data format. Validate that phase-duration observations can be captured with <100ms latency.
Tasking interface specification: latency, reliability, error handling, fallback mechanisms. Validate that corrective action directives can be delivered with <200ms latency.

Safety Services constraint bounds: maximum speed, minimum stopping distance, emergency stop requirements, any phase-duration constraints. Validate that DP2 can allocate phase-duration targets within these bounds for typical site conditions.

Control authority hierarchy specification: explicit statement of NGHE Solution's primary authority, Teleoperation's override conditions, Vehicle firmware's execution-only role. Implement via interface contracts and code review checklist.

Site variability signals inventory: list of observable signals (soil conditions, traffic patterns, weather, load characteristics) and their impact on cycle time. Validate that Telemetry captures all critical signals; document any gaps and their impact on DP2 accuracy.

Instrumentation and logging design: specification of what DP2 and DP4 must log (budget allocation decisions, phase-duration observations, deviations, corrective actions, cycle times), logging format, storage, and analysis tools. Validate that logging overhead is acceptable (<5% latency impact).

Performance validation: end-to-end latency measurement (Telemetry observation → DP4 deviation detection → Tasking directive delivery) under realistic site conditions. Validate that latency budget is met and that p95 ≤ CT_target is achievable.

Failure mode analysis: FMEA or similar analysis of Telemetry, Tasking, and Safety Services failures. Validate that fallback mechanisms (e.g., Vehicle-local governors) prevent safety violations and graceful degradation of cycle-time optimization.
